title: Patrick A. Molligo
text:
I am a Visiting Assistant Professor of Economics at Penn State University (Fall 2026–Spring 2028). My research interests include applied microeconmics and economic history.
I received my Ph.D. in Economics at UCLA in June 2026.

title: Patrick A. Molligo
HBCUs, the G.I. Bill, and Black Educational Attainment
HBCUs, the G.I. Bill, and Black Educational Attainment
text:
Abstract
This paper examines whether access to historically Black colleges and universities affected Black veterans’ ability to use G.I. Bill educational benefits.
Replace this text with the current abstract.

title: HBCUs, the G.I. Bill, and Black Educational Attainment
text:
Results
The preliminary results indicate that G.I. Bill exposure had larger effects on college attendance and educational attainment in areas with greater access to HBCUs, although several estimates are imprecise.

title: HBCUs, the G.I. Bill, and Black Educational Attainment
text:
Citation
Molligo, Patrick A. “HBCUs, the G.I. Bill, and Black Educational Attainment.” Working paper, 2026.

title: The G.I. Bill and Vocational Training
The G.I. Bill and Vocational Training
text:
Abstract
The educational provisions of the World War II and Korean War G.I. Bills dramatically expanded access to postsecondary education and training. This paper examines their effects on vocational-school attendance and completion.
Replace this paragraph with the current abstract from the paper. A paper-page abstract can be longer than the short description used on the Research page.

title: Research
text:
My research lies at the intersection of labor economics, economic history, and the economics of education. I study how public policy and educational institutions shape human-capital investment and labor-market outcomes.







  

    
      
        Job Market Paper
      

      
        2026
      
    

    
      The G.I. Bill and Vocational Training
    

    

    

    
      This paper studies how the educational provisions of the World War II and Korean War G.I. Bills affected vocational training, educational attainment, and long-run labor-market outcomes.
